nameMeanings.name: Reiser
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Hans, Erwin, Kurt, Otto, Hannelore, Heiner, Helmut, Markus, Siegfried, Willi.
German: habitational name for someone from Reis in Bavaria or Reissen in Thuringia (see   Reis   1).
German and Swiss German: occupational name from Middle High German reisære ‘warrior, traveler (on horseback)’.

variants.name: Raiser
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Manfred.
German: variant of   Reiser  .

variants.name: Reisig
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Erna, Gerhard, Heinz.
German: nickname from Middle High German reisec, Middle Low German reisich ‘armed for war’, later ‘sprightly, tall’. See also Reiser 2.

variants.name: Rizer
meanings: Americanized form of German   Reiser  .

variants.name: Ryser
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Hans, Armin, Klaus, Otto, Urs.
Swiss German: variant of   Reiser   1.

variants.name: Rieser
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Aloys, Ernst, Fritz, Hannelore, Hans, Manfred, Ruedi, Volker.  French Norvin, Alain, Marcel.
German:
Alemannic form of   Reiser  .

variants.name: Reisner
meanings: German: probably a habitational name for someone from a place called Reisen in Bavaria, Reissen in Thuringia, or Reussen on the Saale river.
German: variant of   Reiser   2.
German: from an agent derivative of Middle High German, Middle Low German rīse ‘veil’; perhaps an occupational name for someone who made veils.

variants.name: Reis
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Spanish Manuel, Jose, Carlos, Luis, Jorge, Alberto, Julio, Luiz, Alvaro, Francisco, Geraldo, Juanita.  Portuguese Joao, Paulo, Guilherme, Serafim, Afonso, Duarte, Henrique, Joaquim, Marcio.
German: from Middle High German rīs ‘undergrowth, brushwood’; a topographic name for someone who lived in an overgrown area, or a habitational name from Reis or Reissen in Bavaria.
German: occupational name for a cobbler, Middle High German riuze. Compare Reuss .

variants.name: Reisinger
meanings: German:
habitational name for someone from any of several places called Reising or Reisingen in Bavaria.
occupational name for a soldier, from Middle High German reisinc ‘warrior’, literally ‘traveler’ (see Reiser ).

variants.name: Riser
meanings: Swiss German: topographic name for someone who lived near a thicket or underbrush, from an agent noun based on Middle High German rīs ‘branch, shrubbery’.
Possibly an Americanized form of German   Reiser  .

nameMeanings.name: Agnes
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Latinized version of the Greek name Hagnē, from the feminine form of the adjective hagnos ‘pure, holy’. This was the name of a young Roman virgin martyred in the persecutions instigated by the Roman emperor Diocletian in ad 303 . She became a very popular saint in the Middle Ages. Her name was early associated with Latin agnus ‘lamb’, leading to the consistent dropping of the initial H- and to her representation in art accompanied by a lamb. The colloquial form Annes led to some confusion with Ann(e) in earlier centuries. Frequent in the medieval period, the name was revived in the 19th century, and has been especially popular in Scotland. See also Annis .

nameMeanings.name: Agatha
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Pet form of   Agnes   and   Agatha .

variants.name: Agnes
meanings: Latinized version of the Greek name Hagnē, from the feminine form of the adjective hagnos ‘pure, holy’. This was the name of a young Roman virgin martyred in the persecutions instigated by the Roman emperor Diocletian in ad 303 . She became a very popular saint in the Middle Ages. Her name was early associated with Latin agnus ‘lamb’, leading to the consistent dropping of the initial H- and to her representation in art accompanied by a lamb. The colloquial form Annes led to some confusion with Ann(e) in earlier centuries. Frequent in the medieval period, the name was revived in the 19th century, and has been especially popular in Scotland. See also Annis .

en.lifeSummary: Agnes Agatha Reiser was born in 1508, in Bischmisheim, Bischmisheim, Saarbrücken, Rhineland, Prussia. She married Matthis Heimsheimer about 1525, in Württemberg. They were the parents of at least 1 son and 1 daughter.
